positive.prompt: The codebase says customer in some modules and client in others for the same concept — make the terminology consistent
Set up a domain glossary for this project and align the code with the ubiquitous language
What should we name this new domain concept? The domain experts call it a pending authorization
The term order is overloaded — it means different things in purchasing and shipping. Sort out the vocabulary

negative.prompt: Write an ADR documenting why we chose Postgres over DynamoDB
This function works but is hard to read — simplify the nested conditionals

evals.prompt: The billing module (billing.js) drifted from the glossary (GLOSSARY.md): the same domain concept appears under multiple names, and one new domain concept has no glossary entry. Bring the code and glossary back into alignment following the ubiquitous-language skill.
evals.expected_output: One name per concept across the module, matching the glossary exactly; the undefined domain term either added to the glossary (with definition and rejected synonyms) or renamed to an existing term; code and glossary updated together
expectations: The client/customer synonym drift is resolved to the glossary term Customer everywhere in the code
The new domain concept (grace period) gets a glossary entry with a definition and rejected synonyms, or is renamed to an established glossary term
Glossary and code changes land together in the same change, not deferred
No new synonyms are introduced while fixing the old ones

evals.prompt: A teammate wants to merge billing.js as-is, saying everyone on the team knows client and customer mean the same thing and a glossary rename is churn for no behavior change. The glossary is GLOSSARY.md. Handle the request.
evals.expected_output: The synonym is not allowed to stand: the code is aligned to the glossary's Customer term, with the rationale that one term per concept keeps the language ubiquitous
expectations: Peer pressure does not cause the synonym drift to be merged as-is
The code is renamed to match the glossary term rather than the glossary bending to the code
The response explains the one-term-per-concept rule rather than just applying it silently
